records.summary: Semantic-runtime detail-density telemetry is the opt-in shallow sidecar x-ray for product-detail and hot-detail memory representation pressure.
records.rationale: Memory work needs a way to distinguish envelope-handle echoes, non-envelope navigation handles, local keys, string mass, and array mass without recursively walking TypeScript/checker/app object graphs. The detail-density lane intentionally stays shallow and read-only so it can guide product-detail, hot-detail, and query-claim retention decisions without becoming another semantic model.
guidance: Use detail-density when product-detail or hot-detail retention looks suspicious, but treat it as logical payload attribution rather than exact heap ownership.
Keep SemanticRuntimeDetailShapeBuilder as a one-shape work frame: it has a real accumulation lifetime and is clearer as a class than as scattered one-off helper functions.

Keep SemanticRuntimeDetailDensityAccumulator as the per-detail-kind pass accumulator. It owns cross-entry counters and set/map state for one grouped density row; avoid flattening that lifetime into the top-level row reader.

Do not make detail-density recursive by default. Recursing into TypeScript ASTs, checker objects, parent links, or full product graphs would turn a cheap representation x-ray into the memory problem being measured.
When envelope-handle echoes dominate, first check ProductDetailCatalog normalization or detail ownership before dropping navigable fields.

Use handle-kind rows before changing detail classes: address-heavy rows usually point at source precision, product-heavy rows at runtime topology, and identity-heavy hot rows at declaration or owner identity.

HotDetailCatalog hides exact entry-handle echoes such as productHandle; remaining hot-detail handles should be treated as semantic owner/declaration/source lanes unless a durable record can derive them.
When non-envelope handle fields dominate, treat them as navigation topology pressure: move or compress them only after deciding which product, hot detail, or query claim should own that relation.
Keep this telemetry lane opt-in through includeDetailDensity and phase/profile switches; ordinary public answers should not pay for high-cardinality sidecar scans.

Use aggregate phase-memory and phase-kernel rows before deep memory refactors. They show peak construction pressure and phase-owned product volume even when routed answer disposal returns final kernel growth to zero.

Phase kernel breakdowns and phase detail-density must be store-marker/delta based. Do not reintroduce full-store before/after breakdown snapshots inside phase measurement; large apps can have hundreds of nested template phases.

anchors.summary: Aggregates shallow product-detail and hot-detail shape rows by detail kind.
Per-detail-kind accumulator that aggregates direct field, handle-kind, envelope-echo, local-key, and constructor counts into one row.
One-shape work frame that accumulates direct array, string, handle, envelope-echo, and local-key counts.
Opt-in entry point that reads detail-density rows beside kernel count snapshots.
Marker-based phase-local kernel breakdown row reader.
Marker-based phase-local detail-density reader for product details and hot details.
Phase profiler that keeps before/after snapshots cheap and attaches marker-based delta rows when breakdowns are enabled.
Telemetry lane documentation and design rules.

records.summary: The app telemetry profiler is the reusable surface for comparing app-world construction, inquiry projection, query-claim retention, and analysis-depth cost.
records.rationale: Semantic-runtime memory work is a CPU/memory/inquiry-algebra trade-off. The profiler must keep construction phases, public query cost, TypeScript Program composition, query-claim storage, and depth/profile groupings visible before a cache, compression pass, or materialization split is chosen.
guidance: Run this lane before adding cache policy, changing app-opening defaults, trimming product details, or moving work between app construction and public query answers.

Prefer aggregate output for broad pressure, but read the grouped root/depth/profile rows before interpreting global totals. Global totals reveal total load; grouped rows reveal which analysis depth or inquiry profile introduced it.

Use grouped timing leaders when one aggregate run spans multiple depths or profiles. They keep the leading phase costs attached to the root/depth/profile group so a profile comparison does not require immediate narrower reruns. TypeSystem inner phases are part of the aggregate timing lane so Program/checker cost stays visible beside the outer app-world phase.

Use the project compiler-options cache row to separate repeated boot/input wiring from TypeScript Program construction. Hits there show shared tsconfig/path-mapping shape reuse; they do not mean dependency SourceFiles or app-world products were retained.

Treat app-world-free query lanes as first-class canaries. If source/catalog/orientation queries can answer without opening an app world, keep them on the runtime-level claim graph instead of forcing TypeScript Program or template construction.

App-world-free static-evaluation work may surface through answer-envelope telemetry. Interpret `appWorldFreeProfile` as runtime-boundary cost attribution, not as evidence that an app epoch was opened.

Use query-claim telemetry to separate retained answer DTOs, answer-local kernel products, routed app disposal, and TypeScript dependency cache cleanup. The claim graph is the layer immediately before API answers, not another durable semantic model.

Use `SEMANTIC_RUNTIME_TELEMETRY_QUERY_REPEAT` when checking whether the claim graph actually reuses retained answers or whether a public query still rematerializes inside one runtime/app session. `SEMANTIC_RUNTIME_TELEMETRY_REPEAT` repeats whole sessions and answers a different question.

When a product-detail row dominates, pair detail-density with phase/grouped telemetry before changing the representation. Some handle mass is topology or source precision; some is envelope echo that can be normalized away.

anchors.summary: CLI profiler for app-world construction, routed query answers, phase memory/kernel deltas, grouped aggregates, and query-claim retention.
Optional answer-envelope telemetry for runtime-boundary profiling without changing public answer values.
Lazy answer-boundary storage for query outcomes, retained answer values, disposal summaries, and nested query composition.
Public query catalog that declares minimum analysis depth and app-world boundary policy.
Package-level instructions for app telemetry, external pressure hygiene, and depth-first large-app profiling.
Query-claim graph and answer-boundary design notes.
